questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Foreclosure priorities—purchase-money mortgage and prior judgment lien
questions.stem: A buyer contracted to purchase land. Before closing, a creditor obtained and recorded a judgment lien against the buyer covering after-acquired real property. At closing, the seller conveyed the land to the buyer and simultaneously took back a mortgage securing the unpaid purchase price. The seller immediately recorded the purchase-money mortgage. The buyer later defaulted, and both the creditor and seller claimed priority in the land.
questions.call: Whose interest has priority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The creditor, because its judgment lien was recorded before the buyer acquired title.
choices.rationale: A purchase-money mortgage generally receives special priority over prior liens attaching through the purchaser at the instant of acquisition.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The creditor, because the seller recorded the mortgage only after delivery of the deed.
choices.rationale: Simultaneous seller purchase-money financing has priority without the impossible requirement of recording before the purchaser acquires title.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The seller, but only if the creditor had notice of the planned purchase-money mortgage.
choices.rationale: Purchase-money priority generally does not depend on the earlier judgment creditor’s notice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The seller, because the purchase-money mortgage outranks the earlier judgment lien.
choices.rationale: The buyer never obtains an unencumbered beneficial interest ahead of the purchase-money security.

explanation.testedIssue: Priority between a seller’s purchase-money mortgage and an earlier judgment lien against the purchaser.
explanation.governingRule: A purchase-money mortgage generally has priority over liens arising through the purchaser, including earlier judgment liens that attach when the purchaser acquires title, because the mortgage and title acquisition are treated as one transaction.
explanation.application: The seller’s mortgage secured the price and arose simultaneously with conveyance. It therefore outranks the creditor’s after-acquired-property judgment lien.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying a simple first-to-record rule without the purchase-money priority doctrine.
explanation.memoryAid: Purchase money enters first in priority even when another lien waited for title to arrive.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Easement by necessity—prior unity and strict necessity at severance
questions.stem: An owner held a large tract bordered on the north by a public road. The owner conveyed the southern half to a buyer. At the time of conveyance, the southern half had no access to any public road except across the retained northern half. The deed was silent about access. Five years later, the buyer purchased an adjoining parcel to the south that provided a difficult but usable route to another public road. The owner then blocked the original path across the northern half.
questions.call: Does the buyer still have an easement by necessity over the northern half?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the buyer’s later acquisition of usable access ended the necessity.
choices.rationale: The easement arose at severance but is generally extinguished when the strict necessity ceases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because an easement by necessity must be expressly stated in the deed.
choices.rationale: The doctrine supplies an implied easement despite silence when strict necessity and prior unity exist.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because an easement by necessity becomes permanent when created.
choices.rationale: An easement by necessity generally lasts only while the necessity continues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because a difficult alternative route cannot terminate an easement by necessity.
choices.rationale: A usable alternative means of access ordinarily ends strict necessity even if it is less convenient.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an easement by necessity terminates when the dominant owner later obtains alternative access.
explanation.governingRule: An easement by necessity arises when commonly owned land is severed and a parcel is strictly landlocked at severance. It generally continues only so long as the necessity exists.
explanation.application: The southern parcel was landlocked at severance, so an easement arose. The later adjoining purchase supplied usable public-road access and ended the strict necessity.
explanation.commonTrap: Remembering creation requirements but forgetting termination when necessity ceases.
explanation.memoryAid: Necessity creates it; new access ends it.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Recording acts—shelter rule and notice
questions.stem: An owner conveyed land to a buyer, who did not record. The owner later conveyed the same land to a purchaser for value who had no notice of the buyer’s deed. The jurisdiction has a notice statute. The purchaser therefore prevailed over the buyer but also failed to record. The purchaser then gave the land to her brother, who knew about the buyer’s earlier deed. The buyer sued the brother for title.
questions.call: Who prevails?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The buyer, because the brother had notice and did not pay value.
choices.rationale: The shelter rule protects a transferee who takes from a protected bona fide purchaser, even if the transferee has notice or is a donee.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The brother, because the shelter rule gives him the purchaser’s protected title.
choices.rationale: Once the purchaser acquired priority under the notice statute, she could transfer that priority to her brother.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The buyer, because the purchaser failed to record before transferring the land.
choices.rationale: Under a notice statute, the purchaser’s priority arose upon purchase without notice; recording was not required to defeat the earlier unrecorded deed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The brother, but only if he records before the buyer records.
choices.rationale: The jurisdiction is notice, not race-notice, and the brother inherits the purchaser’s priority.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether the shelter rule protects a donee with notice who takes from a bona fide purchaser under a notice statute.
explanation.governingRule: Under the shelter rule, a person who takes from a bona fide purchaser generally succeeds to the purchaser’s recording-act protection even if the transferee had notice or did not give value.
explanation.application: The purchaser acquired priority upon taking for value without notice. Her brother receives that protected title despite his notice and donative status.
explanation.commonTrap: Retesting the transferee’s bona fide purchaser status instead of applying shelter.
explanation.memoryAid: A protected purchaser can shelter later transferees.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Assignment versus sublease and landlord remedies
questions.stem: A tenant held a five-year commercial lease. Two years into the term, the tenant transferred to a retailer the right to possess the entire premises for the remaining three years. The transfer agreement required the retailer to pay rent directly to the landlord but gave the original tenant a contractual right to terminate the retailer’s possession if the retailer failed to maintain the premises. The landlord did not release the tenant. The retailer later stopped paying rent.
questions.call: Which statement best describes the landlord’s rights?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The transfer was a sublease, so the landlord may sue only the original tenant.
choices.rationale: The traditional distinction turns on whether the tenant transferred the entire remaining term, not merely a contractual right of reentry for breach.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The assignment released the original tenant, so the landlord may sue only the retailer.
choices.rationale: Assignment creates privity of estate with the assignee but does not discharge the original tenant’s contractual liability absent novation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The retailer through privity of estate, and the original tenant through privity of contract.
choices.rationale: The entire remaining term was transferred, creating an assignment; both assignee and original tenant may be liable on different bases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The landlord may sue neither party until the five-year lease term expires.
choices.rationale: Each missed installment is ordinarily enforceable when due.

explanation.testedIssue: Classification of a transfer of the entire remaining lease term and resulting liability.
explanation.governingRule: A transfer of the tenant’s entire remaining term is generally an assignment, even if the tenant retains a contractual right of reentry for breach. The assignee is liable on covenants running with the land through privity of estate, while the original tenant remains liable in privity of contract absent novation.
explanation.application: The retailer received all three remaining years, so it is an assignee. The landlord may pursue it for rent while it holds the estate and may also pursue the original tenant on the lease promise.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating a right of reentry as retention of a reversionary estate.
explanation.memoryAid: Entire remaining term = assignment; assignment does not erase the original lease promise.
